title: The Comfort of the Pilgrim
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: The Comfort of the Pilgrim - Pastor Tito Lyro - Psalm 119_49-56.txt
outline: God’s people as pilgrims: Abraham, Moses, Israel in the Promised Land
v.49: Remember your Word to your servant — the basis of hope
v.50: Comfort in affliction through the life-giving Word
vv.51-53: Standing firm against the proud who deride and forsake God’s law
vv.54-56: God’s statutes as songs in the house of pilgrimage
The pilgrim’s treasure: remembering God’s name in the night
summary: Exposition of the Zayin section of Psalm 119 (vv.49-56), centered on the word ‘remember.’ The Bible portrays God’s people as pilgrims and sojourners from Abraham onward, and this stanza gives the pilgrim’s source of comfort in affliction—God’s Word. Despite derision from the proud and the indignation provoked by the wicked, God’s statutes become songs in the house of pilgrimage. The pilgrim’s comfort is not circumstances but the remembered promises of God.
transcript:
## The People of God as Pilgrims

The Bible portrays the people of God as pilgrims and sojourners. Abraham, whom Paul calls the father of the faithful, was a wanderer in this world. He was called out of Ur of the Chaldeans and sent without being told where he was going. His whole life was a pilgrimage. Israel spent forty years living in tents in the desert. And even when they entered the Promised Land, there was always a threat of unsettlement — Philistines, Egyptians, Assyrians. Eventually the Babylonians came and literally took them away to a foreign land.

This pilgrim identity was even built into Israel’s religious calendar. Once a year they left their homes to live in tents during the Feast of Tabernacles, to remember that this world is not their home.

After the captivity, even when Israel returned to the land, they were sojourners in their own country, subjugated by Persians, Greeks, and Romans. The faithful, especially, are called strangers and pilgrims on the earth:

> *“These all died in faith, not having received the promises, but having seen them afar off, were assured of them, embraced them and confessed that they were strangers and pilgrims on the earth… God is not ashamed to be called their God, for He has prepared a city for them.”* (Hebrews 11:13–16)

Peter applies this to us directly: *“Beloved, I beg you as sojourners and pilgrims, abstain from fleshly lusts which war against the soul.”* (1 Peter 2:11)

The writer of Psalm 119 sees himself as a pilgrim: *“Your statutes have been my songs in the house of my pilgrimage.”* (v. 54) His house is this pilgrimage. He does not find his home in this world. And that is where we live — passing through, on our way somewhere else.

## The Pilgrim Wants God to Remember His Promises (Psalm 119:49–50)

> *“Remember the word to Your servant, upon which You have caused me to hope. This is my comfort in my affliction, for Your word has given me life.”* (Psalm 119:49–50)

God is omniscient — He does not forget anything. When the Bible calls us to ask God to *remember*, we are not asking Him to recall something He forgot. We are asking Him to *act* according to what He has said. So when the Psalmist prays, *“Remember Your word to Your servant,”* he is praying: Lord, act according to Your promises.

As pilgrims in this life, we pray:
- Remember, Lord, to be my God and the God of my children.
- Remember, Lord, to never leave me or forsake me.
- Remember, Lord, to be my light and my salvation, the strength of my life.
- Remember, Lord, that Your Son bore my sins on the cross.
- Remember, Lord, to work all things for my good because I love You.
- Remember, Lord, to supply all my needs according to Your riches in glory in Christ Jesus.

We come to God as His servants — the word in verse 49 means *slave* — not as those who deserve anything. And God is faithful to us because He is faithful to Himself, not because we have done something so great that God owes us.

Why does the Psalmist pray this? Because God *caused* him to hope in these promises (v. 49). The dynamic of the Christian life is always: God works, and we work. We see it summarized in Philippians 2:12–13: work out your salvation with fear and trembling, because God is working in you to will and to do His good pleasure. He hopes in God’s Word because of the God of the Word.

Where is your hope today? Our hope is in the Lord, Creator of heaven and earth. Our hope is in the risen Savior who lives to bless His church. The Heidelberg Catechism asks: *“What is your only comfort in life and in death?”* And the answer: *“That I am not my own, but belong with body and soul, in life and in death, to my faithful Savior Jesus Christ.”*

## The Pilgrim Remembers God's Past Dealings (Psalm 119:51–53)

> *“The proud have me in great derision, yet I do not turn aside from Your law. I remember Your judgments of old, O LORD, and have comforted myself. Indignation has taken hold of me because of the wicked who forsake Your law.”* (Psalm 119:51–53)

The Psalmist is suffering at the hands of the proud. He is mocked and held in derision because of his allegiance to God’s Word. But he knows the Word of God, and in it he sees that God has always judged the proud who oppressed His people throughout history.

As Paul says:

> *“Whatever things were written before were written for our learning, that we through the patience and comfort of the Scriptures might have hope.”* (Romans 15:4)

The same comfort is available to us. We read the Bible and see what God does, and knowing He remains the same God, we are comforted and encouraged to stay the course.

We are also comforted by God’s providential care in our personal lives. All of us can look back and point to times when God’s goodness was especially evident — a moment of illumination from the Spirit as you read a passage, an unexpected provision, a smile from a brother or sister when you were discouraged. In his good providence, God shows us He is with us.

And the more we are comforted by God’s care displayed in His Word, the more indignant we become when that Word is defiled. It is proper and even required that we be righteously indignant when the Word of God is disregarded, mocked, or disobeyed.

## The Pilgrim Wants to Obey God (Psalm 119:54–56)

> *“Your statutes have been my songs in the house of my pilgrimage. I remember Your name in the night, O LORD, and I keep Your law. This has become mine, because I kept Your precepts.”* (Psalm 119:54–56)

Life on this earth is to be lived with the constant presence of God’s Word. His statutes are the Psalmist’s songs — always present, always playing. To have the Word always with you means more than carrying a Bible in your pocket. It means to obey the Word.

As the Psalmist lies awake at night, what comes to mind is the name of the Lord — Yahweh, God’s proper name, the ever-present God, the One who entered into covenant with His people. Knowing who God is enables us to keep His law. And obedience produces comfort, hope, and strength — that is what *“this has become mine”* refers to in verse 56.

Brothers and sisters, it is not a burden to obey God’s Word. It is a blessing. Jesus said His yoke is easy and His burden is light — which means the walk apart from Christ is heavy and hard. God has given us His Word to teach us how to live and to show us our Savior. That is not a curse. It is a blessing.

## Conclusion

The walk of the pilgrim has three movements: asking God to remember His promises, remembering what God has done in His Word and in our lives, and obeying God because His law is not a burden but a blessing.

Is Christ your hope in life and in death? Are you holding on to Him for dear life? Is your help in the name of the Lord, the Creator of heaven and earth, who gave us the Lord Jesus Christ to redeem us from our sins and establish us in righteousness?
